explicit_text: My older brother was born in Tule Lake, and my parents were incarcerated there during World War II. My mother went into Tule Lake pregnant, which was a difficult experience given the harsh conditions and limited medical care. We visited Tule Lake recently to understand what they endured, realizing how hard it must have been for her to care for an infant in such a place. Tule Lake was a concentration camp where many Japanese Americans, including my family, were forcibly held after Pearl Harbor.
implicit_text: My older brother was born in the remote camp in northern California, and my parents were incarcerated there during World War II. My mother went into this camp pregnant, which was a difficult experience given the harsh conditions and limited medical care. This location was one of the concentration camps where many Japanese Americans, including my family, were forcibly held after the attack on the naval base in Hawaii.

explicit_text: I was actually born in Caldwell, Idaho, after my parents left Tule Lake. They were sponsored by a farmer to work there, which was how they got out of the camp. Caldwell had a labor camp that was originally built for Dust Bowl migrants, but during the war, Japanese Americans like my parents worked in the sugar beet fields there. My father became a supervisor at the labor camp in Caldwell, and the place still exists today as low-income housing.

explicit_text: My parents got married on November 22, 1941, and just two weeks later, Pearl Harbor was attacked. That event changed everything for Japanese Americans, including my family, who began their married life in a concentration camp. Pearl Harbor was the catalyst for the forced relocation and incarceration of our community during the war.

explicit_text: My mother graduated from the University of Washington with a BA in Home Economics, which was quite an achievement at the time. Her education and skills in sewing and design were important parts of her identity, even though opportunities were limited.

explicit_text: The Amalgamated Sugar Company played a key role in recruiting Japanese Americans from the camps to work in the sugar beet fields during World War II. This company’s efforts allowed my parents and others to leave the camps and work in places like Caldwell, Idaho.

explicit_text: Many young Japanese American men from places like San Francisco and Los Angeles were recruited to work in the sugar beet fields, even though many had no farming experience. San Francisco was one of the urban centers where many Japanese Americans lived before the war and were uprooted.

entity: Presidential Commission on Wartime Relocation and Internment of Civilians
explicit_text: The Presidential Commission on Wartime Relocation and Internment of Civilians was a turning point for many Japanese Americans, including my family. It was only after this commission’s work that people felt it was okay to speak openly about their experiences. My parents and others began to share their stories, and the commission’s findings helped lead to the government’s formal apology and redress.
implicit_text: A federal commission established to investigate wartime relocation and internment was a turning point for many Japanese Americans, including my family. It was only after this commission’s work that people felt it was okay to speak openly about their experiences. My parents and others began to share their stories, and the commission’s findings helped lead to the government’s formal apology and redress.

explicit_text: Grace Uyehara was a remarkable lobbyist in Washington who played a crucial role in the redress movement for Japanese Americans. She was a mover and shaker who brought together coalitions and worked tirelessly to get the government to apologize and provide reparations. Her efforts helped change the course of history for our community.

explicit_text: Judge Marutani was the only Japanese American member on the Presidential Commission on Wartime Relocation and Internment of Civilians. He was from Philadelphia and was part of a strong group of people there who believed in seeking justice and redress. His involvement was important in raising awareness and pushing the commission’s work forward.

Getting his support helped broaden the coalition beyond the West Coast states.

I grew up there, and it was a place where my parents could rebuild their lives after the camps. Auburn was home to our family before and after the war, and it shaped much of my early life.

The Nisei generation faced unique challenges, balancing cultural identity and assimilation during a difficult era.
